---
title: "Notes on Publishing a Quarto Blog with GitHub Pages"
description: "Rendering with Quarto alone does not seem to be enough."
date: "2026-01-23"
draft: true
lang: en
categories: [r, quarto, github]
---

::: {.callout-note}
Original Japanese version: [QuartoでGitHub Pagesにブログを公開するときの注意点](../../../posts/2026-01-23-quarto-github-pages/index.qmd)
:::

This is a note on things to watch when creating a blog with Quarto and publishing it with GitHub Pages.

## Render to `docs/`

For GitHub Pages, the publishing source can be either the repository root or the `docs/` folder.
In Quarto, the default render destination is `_site/`, so the site cannot be published directly through GitHub Pages as-is.
For that reason, the output directory needs to be changed to `docs/`.

In the Quarto configuration file, `_quarto.yml`, specify it as follows.

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs # for GitHub Pages
```

With this setting, Quarto outputs rendered files into the `docs/` folder.

Next, in the GitHub repository settings, set the GitHub Pages source to `docs/`.
Then the Quarto blog can be published with GitHub Pages.

## Add a `.nojekyll` File

GitHub Pages enables a static site generator called Jekyll by default.
Sites created with Quarto are not compatible with Jekyll, so an empty file named `.nojekyll` needs to be added to the `docs/` folder.
This disables Jekyll and allows GitHub Pages to display the Quarto site correctly.

The `.nojekyll` file can be created with the following commands.

- Mac/Linux:

```bash
touch .nojekyll
```

- Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
New-Item .nojekyll -ItemType File
```

- Windows Command Prompt:

```cmd
type nul > .nojekyll
```

Alternatively, you can manually create an empty text file and rename it to `.nojekyll`.

## Use the Publish Command

Quarto also provides the `quarto publish gh-pages` command for publishing directly to GitHub Pages.

## Reference

- [Quarto Documentation - GitHub Pages](https://quarto.org/docs/publishing/github-pages.html)
